witnesses.description: A shortest s-t path of length 5 on a four-node directed weighted graph.
Distance potentials prove that no s-t path is shorter than 5.
A malformed row undercounts the length of the listed path.

checks.claim: The listed path from s to t has exact length 5.
checks.notes: The validator checks that every consecutive vertex pair is a directed edge and sums the edge weights over exact rationals.

checks.claim: The listed path has length 5 and the listed potentials prove no shorter s-t path exists.
checks.notes: The validator recomputes the path length, checks p(s)=0, p(t)=5, and checks p(v) <= p(u)+w(u,v) for every directed edge.

checks.claim: The listed path s-a-b-t has length 4.
checks.notes: Exact path replay recomputes length 5, so the claimed length 4 is rejected.

checks.claim: The same graph has an s-t path of length at most 4.
checks.notes: The potential certificate lower-bounds every s-t path by 5, so a claimed upper bound of 4 is impossible for this finite graph.

checks.claim: The same graph has an s-t path of length at most 4 while the listed potentials lower-bound every s-t path by 5.

data.certificate: produce_lra_evidence emits Evidence::UnsatFarkas checked by Evidence::check for the exact rational inequality conflict 5 <= 4

checks.notes: The SMT-LIB artifact isolates the potential lower-bound contradiction after replay checks the edge relaxations.

checks.claim: General shortest-path algorithm correctness and negative-cycle theory require future theorem-prover reconstruction.

data.target_theorem_shape: For every finite weighted directed graph with no negative s-t improvement cycle, a path plus feasible distance potentials can certify optimality.
data.future_checker: Lean proof of shortest-path optimality, Bellman-Ford/Dijkstra invariants under their hypotheses, and optional all-pairs extensions.

checks.notes: The checked rows cover one explicit graph, one explicit path, and one explicit potential certificate. They do not prove algorithm correctness or asymptotic runtime.
